Camera Trap Analysis

Marc Los Huertos

EA030 · Compiled 2026-04-08

EA030 — RTricks — Project 05

Camera Trap Analysis: Wildlife Detection and Behavior

Prof. Marc Los Huertos  •  Pomona College  •  Environmental Analysis

← Back to Index

Introduction Setup Import & Clean Detection Events Descriptive Behavior × Diel Activity Overlap Diversity Occupancy Summary

What this project covers. Camera traps record who was present, when, and what they were doing without a researcher present. This tutorial walks the complete analytical pipeline — from raw Excel export through cleaning, independent-event definition, descriptive ethograms, and four quantitative methods: chi-square/Fisher tests of diel behavior shifts, kernel-density activity overlap, rarefaction-corrected diversity, and single-season occupancy modeling.

The Analytical Pipeline

Camera-trap data require several decisions before any statistics can be run. The diagram below maps the full workflow covered in this project.

Stage 1
Import & Clean
Read Excel, standardize columns, remove ambiguous taxa, collapse behavior codes.
Stage 2
Independent Events
Apply the 30-minute rule to collapse burst detections into single events. Prevents pseudo-replication.
Stage 3
Descriptive Stats
Ethogram, hourly heat map, camera-level activity profiles.
Stage 4
Hypothesis Testing
Chi-square / Fisher: does behavior composition differ between day and night?
Stage 5
Activity Overlap
Kernel-density overlap coefficient Δ quantifies temporal co-occurrence between species pairs.
Stage 6
Occupancy Model
MacKenzie et al. (2002) separates true absence from non-detection to estimate ψ and p.

🆕 New to R? Don’t worry if every line of code is not immediately clear. Each section explains what the code does and why before showing how to run it. Focus on understanding the logic; the syntax will become familiar with practice. Sections marked with ⚙️ go deeper into the statistical machinery — they are optional on a first reading.

1. Setup

Packages

R’s base functionality is extended by packages — collections of functions written by the community. Install each package once with install.packages(), then load it every session with library().

# Run the install lines once — remove the # to uncomment:
# install.packages(c("tidyverse", "lubridate", "janitor", "readxl",
#                    "vegan", "overlap", "unmarked", "RColorBrewer"))

library(tidyverse)   # data wrangling + ggplot2
library(lubridate)   # date-time helpers
library(janitor)     # clean_names() standardises column headers
library(readxl)      # read Excel files
library(vegan)       # diversity indices and rarefaction
library(overlap)     # kernel-density activity-overlap (Ridout & Linkie 2009)
library(unmarked)    # occupancy models (MacKenzie et al. 2002)

⚙️ Why these packages?
tidyverse — a suite of packages sharing a consistent grammar for data manipulation (dplyr, tidyr) and visualisation (ggplot2).
overlap — implements the Δ estimator of Ridout & Linkie (2009) for circular (time-of-day) kernel densities.
unmarked — fits hierarchical models that separate ecological state (occupancy) from the observation process (detection probability).

2. Data Import and Cleaning

Step 1 Load the data

read_excel() reads a named sheet from an .xlsx file into a data frame — R’s standard rectangular data structure (rows = observations, columns = variables). Update file_path to match your own file location.

file_path <- "/home/mwl04747/RTricks/15_Camera_Traps/Camera Trap Data Entry.xlsx"

cam_raw <- read_excel(path = file_path, sheet = "Combined")

glimpse(cam_raw)
## Rows: 1,197
## Columns: 6
## $ Camera_ID                                 <chr> "P70-03", "P70-03", "P70-03"…
## $ `DateTimeStamp (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS PST)` <chr> "46093.241377314815", "46093…
## $ Datetime_corrected                        <dttm> 2026-03-12 05:47:35, 2026-0…
## $ Taxa_code                                 <chr> "C_latrans", "C_latrans", "C…
## $ Behavior_code                             <chr> "walk", "hop", "walk", "snif…
## $ Notes                                     <chr> NA, NA, "exiting burrow", NA…

💡 glimpse() is your first-look friend. It shows every column name, its data type (<chr> = text, <dbl> = number, <dttm> = date-time), and the first few values — far more useful than head() for wide data frames.

Step 2 Standardize column names and parse dates

Column names from Excel often contain spaces and mixed capitalisation. clean_names() converts them to snake_case (lower case, underscores instead of spaces) so they are easier to type and less error-prone.

cam_raw$datetime <- cam_raw$Datetime_corrected

cam <- cam_raw %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(datetime),   # strip the time component; keep date only
    hour = hour(datetime)       # extract hour of day as integer (0–23)
  )

summary(cam$date)
##         Min.      1st Qu.       Median         Mean      3rd Qu.         Max. 
## "2026-03-09" "2026-03-12" "2026-03-15" "2026-03-15" "2026-03-18" "2026-03-23" 
##         NA's 
##          "1"

Step 3 Remove ambiguous taxa

Some records contain notes like "bird", "unknown", or combined entries ("squirrel/rat"). These are genuinely unidentifiable and must be excluded before analysis — their presence would inflate richness estimates and contaminate behavior summaries.

ambiguous_patterns <- c(
  " and ", " or ", "/",
  "unknown", "unidentified", "n/a", "^na$",
  "bird$", "small brown bird"
)

cam_clean <- cam %>%
  filter(
    !str_detect(
      str_to_lower(taxa_code),
      paste(ambiguous_patterns, collapse = "|")
    )
  )

cat("Taxa retained after filtering:\n")
## Taxa retained after filtering:
unique(cam_clean$taxa_code)
##  [1] "C_latrans"             "A_californica"         "O_beecheyi"           
##  [4] "S_bachmani"            "M_crissalis"           "C_cristata"           
##  [7] "S_niger"               "D_virginiana"          "M_musculus"           
## [10] "Black-headed Grosbeak" "R_norvegicus"          "M_mephitis"

📌 Regular expressions

str_detect() matches text against a pattern. paste(…, collapse = “|”) joins our list of ambiguous strings with the “or” operator (|), so one call checks all of them simultaneously. The ! at the front keeps only rows that do not match any ambiguous pattern.

Step 4 Collapse behaviors into functional types

Raw behavior codes ("walk", "run", "hop") are too granular for most analyses. case_when() groups them into five functional categories — think of it as a multi-way if/else evaluated in order.

cam <- cam_clean %>%
  mutate(
    behavior_function = case_when(
      str_detect(behavior_code, "walk|run|hop|fly|climb|crawl") ~ "movement",
      str_detect(behavior_code, "eat|dig|cache")                ~ "foraging",
      str_detect(behavior_code, "sniff")                        ~ "investigation",
      str_detect(behavior_code, "stare")                        ~ "vigilance",
      str_detect(behavior_code, "stop|stand|sit|lay")           ~ "stationary",
      TRUE                                                      ~ "other"
    )
  )

table(cam$behavior_function)
## 
##      foraging investigation      movement         other    stationary 
##           106            76           754            43           101 
##     vigilance 
##            67

3. Defining Independent Detection Events

The pseudo-replication problem

A single animal may trigger the same camera dozens of times over a few minutes. Treating each trigger as an independent observation would pseudo-replicate the data — inflating sample sizes and biasing every statistic downstream.

The 30-minute rule

The standard solution: any detections of the same species at the same camera within 30 minutes of the previous detection are treated as part of the same event. Only a detection after a ≥ 30-minute gap counts as a new independent event.

cam_events <- cam %>%
  arrange(camera_id, taxa_code, datetime) %>%
  group_by(camera_id, taxa_code) %>%
  mutate(
    # Time gap in minutes between consecutive rows within each camera × species group
    minutes_since_last = as.numeric(
      difftime(datetime, lag(datetime), units = "mins")
    ),
    # A new event begins if gap > 30 min OR this is the very first record
    new_event = if_else(
      is.na(minutes_since_last) | minutes_since_last > 30, 1L, 0L
    ),
    # Cumulative sum gives a running event counter within each group
    event_id = cumsum(new_event)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(diel = if_else(hour >= 6 & hour < 18, "day", "night"))

# How many independent events per species?
cam_events %>%
  distinct(camera_id, taxa_code, event_id) %>%
  count(taxa_code, name = "independent_events") %>%
  arrange(desc(independent_events))
## # A tibble: 12 × 2
##    taxa_code             independent_events
##    <chr>                              <int>
##  1 O_beecheyi                           179
##  2 S_bachmani                           141
##  3 C_latrans                            126
##  4 M_crissalis                           32
##  5 S_niger                               31
##  6 A_californica                         21
##  7 M_musculus                            15
##  8 C_cristata                             3
##  9 Black-headed Grosbeak                  1
## 10 D_virginiana                           1
## 11 M_mephitis                             1
## 12 R_norvegicus                           1

📌 Why lag() is the right tool here

lag(datetime) returns the value from the previous row. Because we first group_by(camera_id, taxa_code), “previous” means the last detection of that species at that specific camera — exactly what we need to measure within-burst gaps. Without grouping, lag() would compare across species and cameras, giving meaningless gaps.

⚠️ Choosing a time threshold

30 minutes is a widely used convention, but the right threshold depends on your study organism’s typical bout length and movement speed. For a chipmunk foraging in a small area, 30 minutes may be too long. For a wide-ranging carnivore like a mountain lion, 60–120 minutes may be more appropriate. Whatever threshold you choose, document it clearly in your methods.

4. Descriptive Statistics

4.1 Detection event totals per taxon

event_summary <- cam_events %>%
  distinct(camera_id, taxa_code, event_id) %>%
  count(taxa_code, name = "detection_events") %>%
  arrange(desc(detection_events))

print(event_summary)
## # A tibble: 12 × 2
##    taxa_code             detection_events
##    <chr>                            <int>
##  1 O_beecheyi                         179
##  2 S_bachmani                         141
##  3 C_latrans                          126
##  4 M_crissalis                         32
##  5 S_niger                             31
##  6 A_californica                       21
##  7 M_musculus                          15
##  8 C_cristata                           3
##  9 Black-headed Grosbeak                1
## 10 D_virginiana                         1
## 11 M_mephitis                           1
## 12 R_norvegicus                         1
ggplot(event_summary,
       aes(x = reorder(taxa_code, detection_events),
           y = detection_events,
           fill = reorder(taxa_code, detection_events))) +
  geom_col(color = "#1e3a2f", alpha = 0.88) +
  geom_text(aes(label = detection_events), hjust = -0.2,
            family = "mono", size = 3.4, color = "#1e3a2f") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = colorRampPalette(c("#d4e6da", "#1e3a2f"))(nrow(event_summary))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(title = "Independent detection events by taxon",
       x = NULL, y = "Detection events") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )

4.2 Behavioral ethogram (stacked bar)

An ethogram documents the relative frequency of each behavior. Proportional bars enable cross-species comparison even when total event counts differ substantially between species.

ethogram <- cam_events %>%
  count(taxa_code, behavior_function) %>%
  group_by(taxa_code) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(ethogram, aes(x = taxa_code, y = prop, fill = behavior_function)) +
  geom_col(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "movement"      = "#4a7c5e",
      "foraging"      = "#c8832a",
      "investigation" = "#2e7d94",
      "vigilance"     = "#8aad96",
      "stationary"    = "#d4e6da",
      "other"         = "#7a7a72"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "Behavioral composition by taxon",
       x = NULL, y = "Proportion of observations")

4.3 Hourly activity heat map

A heat map encodes counts as color intensity. Each cell is a taxon × hour combination — a fast way to spot nocturnal, diurnal, or crepuscular patterns across all taxa simultaneously.

taxon_hour <- cam_events %>%
  distinct(taxa_code, event_id, hour) %>%
  count(taxa_code, hour, name = "events")

ggplot(taxon_hour, aes(x = hour, y = taxa_code, fill = events)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = c(var_cream = "#f7f3ed", "#d4e6da", "#4a7c5e", "#1e3a2f"),
    name    = "Events"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 23, 3),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 23, 3), ":00")) +
  labs(title = "Hourly detection patterns by taxon",
       x = "Hour of day", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9)
  )

4.4 Camera-level activity profiles

Different cameras are placed in different micro-habitats. This plot reveals whether behavioral composition varies by camera location — an early signal of habitat-driven behavior differences.

camera_behavior <- cam_events %>%
  distinct(camera_id, taxa_code, event_id, behavior_function) %>%
  count(camera_id, behavior_function, name = "events")

ggplot(camera_behavior,
       aes(x = camera_id, y = events, fill = behavior_function)) +
  geom_col(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "movement"      = "#4a7c5e",
      "foraging"      = "#c8832a",
      "investigation" = "#2e7d94",
      "vigilance"     = "#8aad96",
      "stationary"    = "#d4e6da",
      "other"         = "#7a7a72"
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "Camera activity profiles by behavior function",
       x = "Camera ID", y = "Detection events")

5. Hypothesis Testing: Behavior × Diel Period

Rationale

Question: Does the proportion of behaviors (e.g., foraging vs. vigilance) differ between day and night?

We use a chi-square test of independence — or Fisher’s exact test when expected cell counts are small — to test whether behavior type is statistically independent of diel period.

H₀: Behavior composition is the same during the day and at night.
H₁: At least one behavior type is more common at one time of day.

⚙️ Chi-square vs. Fisher’s exact test
The chi-square test assumes all expected cell counts ≥ 5. With small samples or rare behaviors, this fails. Fisher’s exact test has no such requirement — it computes an exact p-value by enumerating all possible contingency tables with the same marginal totals. Use Fisher when any expected count is below 5. The code below checks automatically.

Run tests per taxon

test_behavior_diel <- function(df) {

  # 1. Drop rows where diel is NA (records with hour == NA or bad datetime)
  df <- df %>% filter(!is.na(diel), !is.na(behavior_function))

  # 2. Guard: need observations in BOTH diel periods
  if (length(unique(df$diel)) < 2)
    return(tibble(test = "insufficient data — one diel period only",
                  stat_type = "—", statistic = NA_real_, p_value = NA_real_))

  # 3. Build the contingency table
  wide <- df %>%
    count(diel, behavior_function) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from  = behavior_function,
                values_from = n,
                values_fill = 0)

  # 4. Guard: diel column must have no NAs before using as row names
  wide <- wide %>% filter(!is.na(diel))
  if (nrow(wide) < 2)
    return(tibble(test = "insufficient data — pivot produced < 2 rows",
                  stat_type = "—", statistic = NA_real_, p_value = NA_real_))

  tbl <- wide %>%
    column_to_rownames("diel") %>%
    as.matrix()

  # 5. Guard: need at least 2 behavior columns
  if (ncol(tbl) < 2)
    return(tibble(test = "insufficient data — fewer than 2 behavior types",
                  stat_type = "—", statistic = NA_real_, p_value = NA_real_))

  # 6. Choose chi-square vs. Fisher based on minimum expected count
  exp_counts <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(tbl)$expected)
  if (any(exp_counts < 5)) {
    # Fisher's exact test has no single test statistic (no chi-square value).
    # For 2x2 tables it returns an odds ratio; for larger tables use NA.
    res <- fisher.test(tbl, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 2000)
    or_val <- if (!is.null(res$estimate)) round(as.numeric(res$estimate), 3)
              else NA_real_
    tibble(test      = "Fisher",
           statistic = or_val,      # odds ratio when 2x2; NA for larger tables
           stat_type = if (!is.na(or_val)) "odds ratio" else "—",
           p_value   = res$p.value)
  } else {
    res <- chisq.test(tbl)
    tibble(test      = "Chi-square",
           statistic = round(as.numeric(res$statistic), 3),
           stat_type = "X²",
           p_value   = res$p.value)
  }
}

behavior_tests <- cam_events %>%
  group_by(taxa_code) %>%
  group_modify(~ test_behavior_diel(.x)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    significant = ifelse(is.na(p_value), NA, p_value < 0.05),
    p_value     = round(p_value, 4)
  )

# Re-order columns for a readable table
behavior_tests <- behavior_tests %>%
  select(taxa_code, test, stat_type, statistic, p_value, significant)

knitr::kable(
  behavior_tests,
  col.names = c("Taxon", "Test used", "Statistic type",
                "Value", "p-value", "p < 0.05?"),
  caption   = paste0(
    "Diel behavior tests by taxon. ",
    "For chi-square the value is X²; ",
    "for Fisher it is the odds ratio (2×2 tables only; — for larger tables). ",
    "Fisher\'s exact test has no chi-square statistic — ",
    "NA in earlier versions was correct but misleadingly labeled."
  )
)
Diel behavior tests by taxon. For chi-square the value is X²; for Fisher it is the odds ratio (2×2 tables only; — for larger tables). Fisher’s exact test has no chi-square statistic — NA in earlier versions was correct but misleadingly labeled.
Taxon Test used Statistic type Value p-value p < 0.05?
A_californica Fisher — NA 0.0565 FALSE
Black-headed Grosbeak insufficient data — one diel period only — NA NA NA
C_cristata insufficient data — one diel period only — NA NA NA
C_latrans Fisher — NA 0.3523 FALSE
D_virginiana insufficient data — one diel period only — NA NA NA
M_crissalis Fisher — NA 0.0240 TRUE
M_mephitis insufficient data — one diel period only — NA NA NA
M_musculus Fisher — NA 0.1194 FALSE
O_beecheyi Fisher — NA 0.0040 TRUE
R_norvegicus insufficient data — one diel period only — NA NA NA
S_bachmani Fisher — NA 0.0230 TRUE
S_niger Fisher — NA 0.0505 FALSE

Visualize the diel behavior shift

behavior_diel <- cam_events %>%
  # Remove records with no diel assignment or unclassified behavior
  filter(!is.na(diel), !is.na(behavior_function)) %>%
  count(taxa_code, diel, behavior_function) %>%
  group_by(taxa_code, diel) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # Convert diel to a factor with clean labels so NA never appears as a panel
  mutate(
    diel = factor(diel, levels = c("day", "night"),
                  labels = c("Day", "Night"))
  )

# Only keep taxa that have observations in BOTH diel periods
both_periods <- behavior_diel %>%
  distinct(taxa_code, diel) %>%
  count(taxa_code) %>%
  filter(n == 2) %>%
  pull(taxa_code)

behavior_diel <- behavior_diel %>% filter(taxa_code %in% both_periods)

ggplot(behavior_diel,
       aes(x = diel, y = prop, fill = behavior_function)) +
  geom_col(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ taxa_code, ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "movement"      = "#4a7c5e",
      "foraging"      = "#c8832a",
      "investigation" = "#2e7d94",
      "vigilance"     = "#8aad96",
      "stationary"    = "#d4e6da",
      "other"         = "#7a7a72"
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Behavioral composition: day vs. night",
       x = NULL, y = "Proportion", fill = "Behavior") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    strip.text   = element_text(family = "mono",  face = "bold", color = "#2d5a3d"),
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing = unit(0.8, "lines")
  )

💡 Interpreting a significant test A significant p-value tells you that the behavior mix differs between day and night, but not which behaviors drive the difference. Use the faceted stacked bars above to identify which functional types shift most. The chi-square standardized residuals (available in the test object as chisq.test(tbl)$stdres) also pinpoint which cells deviate most from independence.

6. Activity Overlap (Kernel Density)

Rationale

Two species active at the same hours are more likely to interact — as competitors, predators, or prey. The overlap coefficient Δ (Ridout & Linkie 2009) quantifies the similarity of two diel activity curves on a 0–1 scale (0 = no temporal overlap, 1 = identical schedules).

The overlap package fits smooth kernel density curves to detection times treated as a circular variable — because 23:00 and 01:00 are only 2 hours apart, not 22.

Why radians? Putting time on a circle

A standard 24-hour clock has an awkward edge: 23:45 and 00:15 are only 30 minutes apart in reality, but subtracting their decimal hours gives 23.5 — a huge apparent gap. The kernel density estimator would treat those two detections as nearly opposite ends of the day.

The solution is to map the 24-hour cycle onto a circle where midnight wraps seamlessly back to midnight. We do this by converting each detection time to radians (0 to 2π), the natural unit for circular data.

The conversion has three steps:

  1. Decimal hours — combine the hour and fractional minutes into a single number: hour + minute/60. A detection at 14:45 becomes 14.75.
  2. Fraction of the day — divide by 24 to get a value between 0 and 1. 14.75 / 24 = 0.615.
  3. Scale to a full circle — multiply by 2π (≈ 6.283). 0.615 × 2π ≈ 3.86 radians.

⚙️ The full formula
time_rad = (hour + minute/60) / 24 × 2π

Key reference points on the circle:
midnight (00:00) = 0 rad  |  6 am = π/2 ≈ 1.57 rad  |  noon (12:00) = π ≈ 3.14 rad  |  6 pm = 3π/2 ≈ 4.71 rad  |  midnight (24:00) = 2π ≈ 6.28 rad = 0 again

Because 2π and 0 are the same point on the circle, a detection at 23:45 (≈ 6.25 rad) and one at 00:15 (≈ 0.13 rad) are only 0.13 rad apart — correctly close — rather than 6.12 rad apart as a linear scale would report.

cam_events <- cam_events %>%
  mutate(
    # Step 1: decimal hours  →  Step 2: fraction of day  →  Step 3: radians
    time_rad = (hour(datetime) + minute(datetime) / 60) / 24 * 2 * pi
  )

# Sanity check: values should span 0 to ~2π (6.28)
cat(sprintf("time_rad range: %.3f to %.3f  (expected 0 to %.3f)\n",
            min(cam_events$time_rad, na.rm = TRUE),
            max(cam_events$time_rad, na.rm = TRUE),
            2 * pi))
## time_rad range: 0.105 to 6.165  (expected 0 to 6.283)

Single pair: estimate Δ and bootstrap CI

# ── Minimum detections needed for bandwidth estimation ────────────────────────
MIN_N_OVERLAP <- 10   # raise further if bandwidth errors persist

# Helper: attempt overlap analysis, return NULL gracefully on failure
run_overlap <- function(ta, tb, sp1, sp2) {
  if (length(ta) < MIN_N_OVERLAP || length(tb) < MIN_N_OVERLAP) {
    message(sprintf("Skipping %s vs %s: too few detections (%d, %d).",
                    sp1, sp2, length(ta), length(tb)))
    return(NULL)
  }
  if (length(unique(ta)) < 5 || length(unique(tb)) < 5) {
    message(sprintf("Skipping %s vs %s: too few unique time values.", sp1, sp2))
    return(NULL)
  }
  estimator <- if (min(length(ta), length(tb)) < 75) "Dhat4" else "Dhat1"
  tryCatch({
    delta_val <- overlapEst(ta, tb, type = estimator)[estimator]
    set.seed(42)
    bs_val <- bootstrap(ta, tb, nb = 999, type = estimator)
    ci_val <- bootCI(delta_val, bs_val)
    list(estimator = estimator, delta = delta_val,
         ci_lower  = ci_val["norm0", "lower"],
         ci_upper  = ci_val["norm0", "upper"])
  }, error = function(e) {
    message(sprintf("Overlap failed for %s vs %s: %s", sp1, sp2, conditionMessage(e)))
    NULL
  })
}

# ── Pick the two species with the most independent detections ─────────────────
top2 <- cam_events %>%
  distinct(taxa_code, event_id) %>%
  count(taxa_code, sort = TRUE) %>%
  slice_head(n = 2) %>%
  pull(taxa_code)

sp1_code <- top2[1]
sp2_code <- top2[2]

t1 <- cam_events %>% filter(taxa_code == sp1_code) %>% pull(time_rad)
t2 <- cam_events %>% filter(taxa_code == sp2_code) %>% pull(time_rad)

ov <- run_overlap(t1, t2, sp1_code, sp2_code)

if (!is.null(ov)) {
  cat(sprintf("Activity overlap (%s):  %s vs. %s  \u2192  \u0394 = %.3f\n",
              ov$estimator, sp1_code, sp2_code, ov$delta))
  cat(sprintf("Bootstrap 95%% CI: [%.3f, %.3f]\n", ov$ci_lower, ov$ci_upper))
  overlapPlot(
    t1, t2,
    rug        = TRUE,
    main       = paste("Activity overlap:", sp1_code, "vs.", sp2_code),
    xlab       = "Time of day",
    linecol    = "#4a7c5e",
    linecol2   = "#c8832a",
    linewidth  = 2,
    linewidth2 = 2
  )
  legend("topright", legend = c(sp1_code, sp2_code),
         col = c("#4a7c5e", "#c8832a"), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
} else {
  # Fallback: plain hourly histograms when KDE cannot run
  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))
  hist(t1 / (2*pi) * 24, breaks = 24, col = adjustcolor("#4a7c5e", 0.7),
       border = "white", xlab = "Hour of day", main = sp1_code, xlim = c(0,24))
  hist(t2 / (2*pi) * 24, breaks = 24, col = adjustcolor("#c8832a", 0.7),
       border = "white", xlab = "Hour of day", main = sp2_code, xlim = c(0,24))
  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
  message("KDE overlap unavailable — showing hourly histograms instead.")
}
## Activity overlap (Dhat1):  O_beecheyi vs. S_bachmani  →  Δ = 0.627
## Bootstrap 95% CI: [0.565, 0.690]

📌 Interpreting Δ

Δ > 0.75 = high overlap; 0.50–0.75 = moderate; < 0.50 = low. If the bootstrap CI excludes a reference value of interest (e.g., Δ = 0.5), the difference is statistically supported. See Ridout & Linkie (2009) for formal guidance on estimator choice and CI interpretation.

All pairwise overlaps (heat map)

taxa_list <- unique(cam_events$taxa_code)
n_sp      <- length(taxa_list)

time_by_sp <- setNames(
  lapply(taxa_list, function(sp)
    cam_events %>% filter(taxa_code == sp) %>% pull(time_rad)),
  taxa_list
)

# Helper: safe single-pair overlap (returns NA on any failure)
safe_overlap <- function(ta, tb) {
  if (length(ta) < MIN_N_OVERLAP || length(tb) < MIN_N_OVERLAP) return(NA_real_)
  if (length(unique(ta)) < 5    || length(unique(tb)) < 5)    return(NA_real_)
  est <- if (min(length(ta), length(tb)) < 75) "Dhat4" else "Dhat1"
  tryCatch(
    overlapEst(ta, tb, type = est)[est],
    error = function(e) NA_real_
  )
}

# Fill the symmetric overlap matrix, computing each pair only once
overlap_mat <- matrix(NA, n_sp, n_sp,
                      dimnames = list(taxa_list, taxa_list))
for (i in seq_len(n_sp)) {
  for (j in seq_len(n_sp)) {
    if (i == j) {
      overlap_mat[i, j] <- 1
    } else if (!is.na(overlap_mat[j, i])) {
      overlap_mat[i, j] <- overlap_mat[j, i]   # mirror already computed
    } else {
      overlap_mat[i, j] <- safe_overlap(time_by_sp[[i]], time_by_sp[[j]])
    }
  }
}

overlap_df <- as.data.frame(overlap_mat) %>%
  rownames_to_column("sp1") %>%
  pivot_longer(-sp1, names_to = "sp2", values_to = "delta")

ggplot(overlap_df, aes(x = sp1, y = sp2, fill = delta)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(delta, 2)), size = 3,
            color = ifelse(overlap_df$delta > 0.6, "white", "#1a1a18")) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = c("#f7f3ed", "#d4e6da", "#4a7c5e", "#1e3a2f"),
    limits  = c(0, 1), name = "Δ"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Pairwise activity overlap (Δ)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1)
  )

7. Species Diversity and Rarefaction

Rationale

Some cameras may be in richer habitats and detect more species. But cameras also vary in effort — a busier camera accumulates more detections by chance, inflating apparent diversity. Rarefaction corrects for this by asking: how many species would we expect if all cameras had the same total event count?

Build the site × species matrix

# One row per camera, one column per taxon, cell = independent event count
site_sp <- cam_events %>%
  distinct(camera_id, taxa_code, event_id) %>%
  count(camera_id, taxa_code, name = "events") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from  = taxa_code,
              values_from = events,
              values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("camera_id")

print(site_sp)
##         A_californica Black-headed Grosbeak C_latrans M_crissalis M_musculus
## P70-01              8                     1         3          18         14
## P70-02              1                     0        17          11          0
## P70-03              5                     0         7           0          0
## P70-04              0                     0        38           0          0
## T005                2                     0         7           0          0
## T20-01              0                     0         0           0          0
## T20-03              0                     0        27           2          0
## T20-04              2                     0        19           0          0
## TC22-01             1                     0         1           0          1
## TC29-01             2                     0         7           1          0
##         O_beecheyi R_norvegicus S_bachmani S_niger M_mephitis C_cristata
## P70-01          11            1         24       2          0          0
## P70-02          59            0         52       0          0          0
## P70-03           1            0          1       0          0          0
## P70-04           1            0          0       2          0          0
## T005             5            0          5       0          0          0
## T20-01          20            0          0       0          1          0
## T20-03           4            0         26       0          0          3
## T20-04          27            0         12       5          0          0
## TC22-01         22            0          0       3          0          0
## TC29-01         29            0         21      19          0          0
##         D_virginiana
## P70-01             0
## P70-02             0
## P70-03             0
## P70-04             0
## T005               0
## T20-01             0
## T20-03             0
## T20-04             0
## TC22-01            0
## TC29-01            1

Diversity indices

div_df <- tibble(
  camera_id    = rownames(site_sp),
  richness     = specnumber(site_sp),
  shannon      = diversity(site_sp, "shannon"),
  simpson      = diversity(site_sp, "simpson"),
  total_events = rowSums(site_sp)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(shannon))

knitr::kable(div_df,
             digits  = 3,
             col.names = c("Camera", "Richness (S)", "Shannon (H′)",
                           "Simpson (1−D)", "Total events"),
             caption = "Diversity indices by camera. Rarefied richness (below) controls for effort differences.")
Diversity indices by camera. Rarefied richness (below) controls for effort differences.
Camera Richness (S) Shannon (H′) Simpson (1−D) Total events
P70-01 9 1.810 0.807 82
TC29-01 7 1.475 0.735 80
T20-04 5 1.341 0.701 65
T005 4 1.307 0.715 19
P70-02 5 1.223 0.663 140
T20-03 5 1.161 0.627 62
P70-03 4 1.091 0.612 14
TC22-01 5 0.786 0.367 28
P70-04 3 0.308 0.138 41
T20-01 2 0.191 0.091 21 
div_df %>%
  pivot_longer(c(richness, shannon, simpson),
               names_to = "index", values_to = "value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = camera_id, y = value, fill = camera_id)) +
  geom_col(color = "#1e3a2f", alpha = 0.85, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ index, scales = "free_y",
             labeller = labeller(index = c(
               richness = "Richness (S)",
               shannon  = "Shannon (H′)",
               simpson  = "Simpson (1−D)"
             ))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = colorRampPalette(c("#d4e6da", "#1e3a2f"))(nrow(div_df))
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1),
    strip.text  = element_text(family = "mono", face = "bold", color = "#2d5a3d")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Diversity indices by camera", x = "Camera ID", y = "Index value")

📌 Which diversity index to report?

Richness (S) — counts species, ignores abundance; sensitive to sampling effort.
Shannon (H′) — weights common and rare species equally on a log scale; the most widely reported index in ecology.
Simpson (1−D) — weighted toward dominant species; less sensitive to rare taxa.

Report all three and discuss what differences reveal about community structure. High S with low H′ indicates one highly dominant taxon with many rare ones.

Rarefaction curves

The problem rarefaction solves. Camera A recorded 80 independent events and detected 6 species. Camera B recorded 12 events and detected 4 species. Is Camera A genuinely richer, or did it simply run longer and accumulate more detections by chance? Raw species counts cannot answer this — they confound ecological richness with sampling effort.

What a rarefaction curve does. For each camera, the vegan function rarecurve() randomly sub-samples its detection records 1, 2, 3 … n times and asks: on average, how many species had we seen after exactly k detections? The result is a curve of expected richness as a function of effort. Plotting all cameras on the same axes — with the same x-scale — makes a fair comparison possible: read off each curve at a common x-value (the rarefaction target, set to the smallest total across all cameras) to get effort-controlled richness.

How to read the plot. Each coloured line is one camera. The vertical dashed line marks the rarefaction target — the point where all cameras can be compared fairly. At that x-value, a higher curve means genuinely greater local richness, not just more deployment time.

  • Steep and still rising at the target → the camera is under-sampled; more events would likely reveal additional species.
  • Levelling off (asymptote) before the target → the local species pool is well-characterized; additional effort would add little.
  • Short curves that barely reach the target → those cameras had very few total events and their richness estimates are highly uncertain.
min_n <- min(rowSums(site_sp))
cat(sprintf(
  "Rarefaction target: %d detections (the minimum across %d cameras)\n",
  min_n, nrow(site_sp)
))
## Rarefaction target: 14 detections (the minimum across 10 cameras)
cat(sprintf(
  "Cameras with more than 2× the target: %d\n",
  sum(rowSums(site_sp) > 2 * min_n)
))
## Cameras with more than 2× the target: 6
# Build a color palette with one color per camera
cam_cols <- colorRampPalette(
  RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(8, "Dark2")
)(nrow(site_sp))

rarecurve(
  site_sp,
  step    = 1,
  col     = cam_cols,
  lwd     = 1.8,
  xlab    = "Detection events (sampling effort)",
  ylab    = "Expected species richness",
  main    = "Rarefaction curves by camera",
  label   = TRUE,
  cex     = 0.75
)
abline(v = min_n, lty = 2, col = "#7a7a72", lwd = 1.5)
text(min_n, par("usr")[3] + 0.3,
     paste0("Rarefaction target = ", min_n, " events"),
     adj = c(-0.08, 0), cex = 0.8, col = "#7a7a72")

# Effort-controlled richness at the rarefaction target for each camera
rare_vals <- rarefy(site_sp, sample = min_n)

rarefied_df <- tibble(
  Camera            = names(rare_vals),
  `Raw richness`    = specnumber(site_sp),
  `Total events`    = rowSums(site_sp),
  `Rarefied S`      = round(rare_vals, 2),
  `Rank (raw)`      = rank(-specnumber(site_sp), ties.method = "min"),
  `Rank (rarefied)` = rank(-rare_vals, ties.method = "min")
) %>% arrange(`Rank (rarefied)`)

knitr::kable(
  rarefied_df,
  caption = paste0(
    "Effort-controlled species richness rarefied to ", min_n,
    " detection events per camera. ",
    "Cameras whose rarefied rank differs from their raw rank had ",
    "apparent richness inflated (or deflated) by effort differences."
  ),
  align = "lrrrrr"
)
Effort-controlled species richness rarefied to 14 detection events per camera. Cameras whose rarefied rank differs from their raw rank had apparent richness inflated (or deflated) by effort differences.
Camera Raw richness Total events Rarefied S Rank (raw) Rank (rarefied)
P70-01 9 82 5.69 1 1
TC29-01 7 80 4.40 2 2
T20-04 5 65 4.06 3 3
P70-03 4 14 4.00 7 4
T005 4 19 3.94 7 5
P70-02 5 140 3.65 3 6
T20-03 5 62 3.60 3 7
TC22-01 5 28 3.39 3 8
P70-04 3 41 1.91 9 9
T20-01 2 21 1.67 10 10

💡 Rarefied S vs. raw richness — when do they disagree?

If a camera’s raw rank is higher than its rarefied rank, its raw species count was inflated by extra effort — it detected more species simply because it ran longer, not because its habitat is richer. The rarefied value is the fairer number to report in any cross-camera comparison. Note that rarefaction always reduces or maintains richness relative to the raw count — it can never add species that were not observed.

8. Single-Season Occupancy Modeling

Rationale and key insight

Which cameras does species X truly occupy? The complication is imperfect detection: even when a species is present, a camera will not always fire. Treating “no detection” as “truly absent” underestimates occupancy — sometimes dramatically.

The MacKenzie et al. (2002) model simultaneously estimates:

  • ψ (psi) — the probability a site is occupied.
  • p — the probability of detecting the species on a given survey occasion, given it is present.

We split the monitoring week into daily occasions. For each camera × day, the detection history is 1 (detected) or 0 (not detected).

⚙️ The occupancy likelihood
For a site with detection history [1, 0, 1]:
P(data) = ψ × p(1−p)p + (1−ψ) × 0
For a history of all zeros [0, 0, 0]:
P(data) = ψ × (1−p)³ + (1−ψ)
The second term captures the possibility the site was unoccupied — this is what separates a true absence from a detection failure.

Build detection histories

focal_sp    <- unique(cam_events$taxa_code)[1]
cat("Focal species:", focal_sp, "\n")
## Focal species: A_californica
all_dates   <- sort(unique(cam_events$date))
all_cameras <- sort(unique(cam_events$camera_id))

# 1 = species detected that camera × day; 0 = not detected
det_wide <- cam_events %>%
  filter(taxa_code == focal_sp) %>%
  distinct(camera_id, date) %>%
  mutate(detected = 1) %>%
  complete(camera_id = all_cameras, date = all_dates,
           fill = list(detected = 0)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = date, values_from = detected) %>%
  column_to_rownames("camera_id")

print(det_wide)
##         2026-03-09 2026-03-10 2026-03-11 2026-03-12 2026-03-13 2026-03-14
## P70-01           1          1          0          1          0          1
## P70-02           0          0          0          0          0          0
## P70-03           0          0          0          0          0          0
## P70-04           0          0          0          0          0          0
## T005             0          0          0          1          0          1
## T20-01           0          0          0          0          0          0
## T20-03           0          0          0          0          0          0
## T20-04           0          0          0          0          1          1
## TC22-01          0          0          0          0          0          0
## TC29-01          0          0          0          0          0          0
##         2026-03-15 2026-03-16 2026-03-17 2026-03-18 2026-03-19 2026-03-20
## P70-01           0          0          0          0          0          0
## P70-02           0          0          0          0          0          0
## P70-03           1          0          1          1          0          1
## P70-04           0          0          0          0          0          0
## T005             0          0          0          0          0          0
## T20-01           0          0          0          0          0          0
## T20-03           0          0          0          0          0          0
## T20-04           0          0          0          0          0          0
## TC22-01          1          0          0          0          0          0
## TC29-01          0          0          1          1          0          0
##         2026-03-21 2026-03-22 2026-03-23
## P70-01           0          0          0
## P70-02           0          1          0
## P70-03           0          0          0
## P70-04           0          0          0
## T005             0          0          0
## T20-01           0          0          0
## T20-03           0          0          0
## T20-04           0          0          0
## TC22-01          0          0          0
## TC29-01          0          0          0

Fit the null occupancy model

umf      <- unmarkedFrameOccu(y = as.matrix(det_wide))
fit_null <- occu(~ 1 ~ 1, data = umf)
summary(fit_null)
## 
## Call:
## occu(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf)
## 
## Occupancy (logit-scale):
##  Estimate   SE    z P(>|z|)
##      1.32 1.06 1.25   0.212
## 
## Detection (logit-scale):
##  Estimate    SE     z  P(>|z|)
##     -1.86 0.321 -5.78 7.47e-09
## 
## AIC: 104.4236 
## Number of sites: 10
# Back-transform from logit scale to probability
occ_est <- backTransform(fit_null, type = "state")
det_est <- backTransform(fit_null, type = "det")

cat(sprintf(
  "\nEstimated occupancy  ψ = %.3f  (95%% CI: %.3f – %.3f)\n",
  occ_est@estimate,
  confint(occ_est)[1], confint(occ_est)[2]
))
## 
## Estimated occupancy  ψ = 0.789  (95% CI: 0.320 – 0.968)
cat(sprintf(
  "Estimated detection  p = %.3f  (95%% CI: %.3f – %.3f)\n",
  det_est@estimate,
  confint(det_est)[1], confint(det_est)[2]
))
## Estimated detection  p = 0.135  (95% CI: 0.077 – 0.227)

Compare naïve vs. model-estimated occupancy

naive_occ <- mean(rowSums(det_wide) > 0)

cat(sprintf(
  "Naïve occupancy (fraction of cameras with ≥1 detection): %.3f\n", naive_occ
))
## Naïve occupancy (fraction of cameras with ≥1 detection): 0.700
cat(sprintf(
  "Model-estimated occupancy ψ:                             %.3f\n",
  occ_est@estimate
))
## Model-estimated occupancy ψ:                             0.789
cat(sprintf(
  "Inflation due to imperfect detection:                    +%.3f\n",
  occ_est@estimate - naive_occ
))
## Inflation due to imperfect detection:                    +0.089

Covariate model: does vegetation density affect occupancy?

If you have a site-level covariate (e.g., canopy cover measured at each camera), you can test whether occupancy varies with habitat quality. Replace the simulated veg_density below with your real covariate.

# ── Replace this block with your real covariate data ────────────────────────
set.seed(7)
site_covs <- data.frame(
  veg_density = scale(runif(nrow(det_wide), 0, 100))
)
# ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

umf_cov <- unmarkedFrameOccu(
  y        = as.matrix(det_wide),
  siteCovs = site_covs
)

fit_cov <- occu(~ 1 ~ veg_density, data = umf_cov)
summary(fit_cov)
## 
## Call:
## occu(formula = ~1 ~ veg_density, data = umf_cov)
## 
## Occupancy (logit-scale):
##             Estimate   SE     z P(>|z|)
## (Intercept)    1.351 1.11 1.215   0.224
## veg_density    0.357 1.14 0.314   0.754
## 
## Detection (logit-scale):
##  Estimate    SE     z  P(>|z|)
##     -1.86 0.321 -5.79 7.25e-09
## 
## AIC: 106.3112 
## Number of sites: 10
# AIC comparison
aic_df <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Null  (ψ(.), p(.))",
            "Veg   (ψ(veg), p(.))"),
  AIC   = round(c(fit_null@AIC, fit_cov@AIC), 2),
  dAIC  = round(c(0, fit_cov@AIC - fit_null@AIC), 2)
)
knitr::kable(
  aic_df,
  col.names = c("Model", "AIC", "ΔAIC"),
  align     = "lcc",
  caption   = paste0(
    "AIC model comparison. The model with lower AIC is better supported. ",
    "ΔAIC > 2 is the conventional threshold for meaningful improvement. ",
    "ΔAIC < 2 means the simpler null model is preferred."
  )
)
AIC model comparison. The model with lower AIC is better supported. ΔAIC > 2 is the conventional threshold for meaningful improvement. ΔAIC < 2 means the simpler null model is preferred.
Model AIC ΔAIC
Null (ψ(.), p(.)) 104.42 0.00
Veg (ψ(veg), p(.)) 106.31 1.89

📌 Interpreting AIC

The model with the lower AIC has better predictive support. ΔAIC > 2 is the conventional threshold for “meaningfully better”. A positive coefficient on veg_density in the occupancy sub-model means the species is more likely to occupy densely vegetated sites. AIC penalizes complexity — a more parameterized model must fit considerably better to justify its extra parameters.

Predicted occupancy across the vegetation gradient

The covariate model estimated a single coefficient for veg_density on the logit (log-odds) scale. That number is hard to interpret directly — a coefficient of +0.8 does not mean “occupancy increases by 0.8.” The logit link is non-linear, so the same unit increase in vegetation density produces a larger change in ψ near 0.50 than it does near 0 or 1.

predict() translates the coefficient back into probability space across the full observed range of the covariate, producing the response curve — the most interpretable summary of the model. The code below:

  1. Creates a prediction grid — 100 evenly spaced values of veg_density spanning the observed range (from the most open to the densest site).
  2. Calls predict(type = "state") — "state" means the occupancy sub-model (ψ), as opposed to "det" which would give the detection sub-model (p). The function returns four columns: Predicted (ψ̂), SE (standard error on the probability scale), and lower/upper (95% confidence interval bounds).
  3. Plots the curve and ribbon — the shaded band is the 95% CI, reflecting uncertainty in the estimated coefficient. Wide bands indicate either a small sample of cameras or weak signal in the data.
# Step 1: prediction grid across the full covariate range
newdat <- data.frame(
  veg_density = seq(min(site_covs$veg_density),
                    max(site_covs$veg_density),
                    length.out = 100)
)

# Step 2: predict ψ at each grid point (back-transformed to probability scale)
pred <- predict(fit_cov, type = "state", newdata = newdat)

# Show what predict() returns — four columns
head(round(pred, 3))
##   Predicted    SE lower upper
## 1     0.722 0.284 0.140 0.976
## 2     0.724 0.278 0.146 0.976
## 3     0.726 0.273 0.152 0.975
## 4     0.728 0.268 0.158 0.974
## 5     0.730 0.263 0.164 0.974
## 6     0.731 0.259 0.171 0.973
# Step 3: plot the response curve
pred_df <- bind_cols(newdat, pred)

# Annotate where ψ = 0.5 (the inflection point of the logistic curve)
psi50_x <- pred_df$veg_density[which.min(abs(pred_df$Predicted - 0.5))]
psi50_y <- 0.5

ggplot(pred_df, aes(x = veg_density, y = Predicted)) +
  # 95% CI ribbon
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper),
              fill = "#d4e6da", alpha = 0.75) +
  # Response curve
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a2f", linewidth = 1.4) +
  # ψ = 0.5 reference lines
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "#c8832a", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = psi50_x, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "#c8832a", linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("point", x = psi50_x, y = psi50_y,
           shape = 21, size = 3.5, fill = "#c8832a", color = "white") +
  annotate("text",
           x     = psi50_x + 0.08 * diff(range(pred_df$veg_density)),
           y     = 0.53,
           label = sprintf("ψ = 0.50
at veg = %.2f", psi50_x),
           color = "#c8832a", size = 3.2, hjust = 0, fontface = "italic") +
  # Rug: actual camera covariate values
  geom_rug(data = data.frame(veg_density = site_covs$veg_density),
           aes(x = veg_density), y = NULL,
           sides = "b", color = "#4a7c5e", alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1),
                     labels = scales::percent_format(),
                     breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.25)) +
  labs(
    title    = paste("Predicted occupancy (ψ) for", focal_sp),
    subtitle = "Shaded band = 95% CI  |  Rug marks = actual camera locations  |  Orange lines = ψ = 0.50",
    x        = "Vegetation density (standardized z-score)",
    y        = "Estimated occupancy probability (ψ)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(family = "serif", face = "bold", color = "#1e3a2f"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, color = "#7a7a72")
  )

📌 Reading the response curve — four questions to ask

1. Direction: Does ψ increase or decrease with the covariate? A rising curve means the species is more likely to occupy sites with more vegetation; a falling curve means the opposite.

2. Magnitude: How much does ψ change across the full covariate range? If ψ only moves from 0.42 to 0.51, the effect is statistically detectable but ecologically trivial. If it moves from 0.10 to 0.85, habitat selection is strong.

3. Uncertainty: How wide is the shaded band? Wide CIs at the covariate extremes mean few cameras were sampled there — interpret the predicted ψ at those values with caution.

4. Inflection point: The orange dot marks ψ = 0.50, the covariate value at which occupancy and non-occupancy are equally likely. This is the logistic regression analogue of an LC50 — a useful threshold for management (e.g., “sites with vegetation density above X are more likely than not to be occupied”).

⚠️ This example uses simulated covariate data

The veg_density values above were generated with runif() and have no relationship to the actual cameras. The curve shape and inflection point are therefore meaningless for your real focal species. Replace the simulation block with measured field covariates — canopy cover from a densiometer, distance to water, trail-camera height class, or any site-level variable you recorded — before drawing ecological conclusions.

9. Summary and Discussion

Full camera-trap analysis pipeline.
Stage Key idea R functions
Import & clean Remove ambiguous taxa; standardize columns with clean_names() read_excel(), clean_names(), case_when()
Independent events 30-minute rule collapses burst detections; prevents pseudo-replication lag(), difftime(), cumsum()
Descriptive stats Ethograms, heat maps, and camera profiles reveal community-level patterns ggplot2: geom_col(), geom_tile()
Chi-square / Fisher Tests whether behavior composition shifts between day and night chisq.test(), fisher.test()
Activity overlap Δ Kernel density Δ quantifies temporal co-occurrence on a 0–1 scale overlapEst(), bootstrap(), bootCI()
Rarefaction Controls for unequal detection effort before comparing camera richness rarecurve(), specnumber(), diversity()
Occupancy model Separates true absence from non-detection to estimate ψ and p occu(), backTransform(), predict()

💬 Discussion Questions

  1. Which taxon showed the greatest day–night behavioral shift? What ecological mechanism might explain it — predation pressure, thermoregulation, or resource availability?
  2. Do species with high activity overlap (Δ > 0.75) tend to be ecologically similar (same guild) or different? What does this suggest about competitive exclusion vs. temporal partitioning?
  3. Examine the rarefaction curves. Which cameras appear under-sampled (curves not levelled off)? How would you redesign the study to address this — longer deployment, more cameras, or both?
  4. Compare the naïve detection rate for your focal species with the model-estimated occupancy ψ. How large is the imperfect-detection bias? Does it change any substantive conclusions about habitat use?
  5. The covariate model used a simulated vegetation density variable. What real site covariates would you collect in the field, and how would you measure them consistently across cameras?

🔑 Key takeaways

  • Always define independent events before analysis. The 30-minute rule is a convention — document your threshold and justify it relative to your study organism.
  • Descriptive plots come first. An ethogram or heat map often surfaces the most important ecological patterns before any test is run.
  • Chi-square vs. Fisher: check expected counts with chisq.test()$expected; switch to fisher.test() automatically when any cell is below 5.
  • Activity overlap Δ is sample-size sensitive. Use Dhat4 when either species has fewer than 75 detections; always report a bootstrap CI alongside the point estimate.
  • Rarefaction, not raw richness, is the correct basis for comparing cameras with unequal total events.
  • Occupancy models separate biology from observation. Naïve detection rate systematically underestimates true occupancy when detection is imperfect — which it always is with camera traps.

References

  • MacKenzie, D. I., Nichols, J. D., Lachman, G. B., Droege, S., Royle, J. A., & Langtimm, C. A. (2002). Estimating site occupancy rates when detection probabilities are less than one. Ecology, 83(8), 2248–2255.
  • Ridout, M. S., & Linkie, M. (2009). Estimating overlap of daily activity patterns from camera trap data. Journal of Agricultural, Biological, and Environmental Statistics, 14(3), 322–337.
  • Fiske, I., & Chandler, R. (2011). unmarked: An R package for fitting hierarchical models of wildlife occurrence and abundance. Journal of Statistical Software, 43(10), 1–23.
  • Oksanen, J., et al. (2022). vegan: Community Ecology Package. R package version 2.6-4.
